coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted one-pot synthesis of graphene quantum dots as highly sensitive fluorescent probes for detection of iron ions and pH value
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Chunfang
Cui, Yanyan
Song, Li
Liu, Xiangfeng
Hu, Zhongbo
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon nanomaterials have received considerable attention as fluorescent probes owing to their low toxicity, water solubility and stable photochemical properties. However, the development of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is still on its early stage. In this work, GQDs were successfully synthesized by one-step microwave assisted pyrolysis of aspartic acid (Asp) and NH4HCO3 mixture. The as-prepared GQDs exhibited strongly blue fluorescence with high quantum yield up to 14%. Strong fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on GQDs can be used for its high selectivity detection among of general metal ions. The probe exhibited a wide linear response concentration range (0–50μM) to Fe3+ and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.26μM. In addition, GQDs are also sensitive to the pH value in the range from 2 to 12 indicating a great potential as optical pH sensors. More importantly, the GQDs possess lower cellular toxicity and high photostability and can be directly used as fluorescent probes for cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Sensor
Detection of Fe3+
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of metal ions and pH on the PL of the GQDs
Fluorescence response of GQDs toward pH
Fluorescence response of GQDs on Fe3+
Cytotoxicity of GQDs and the application in cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical TEM image (A), HRTEM image (B), FTIR spectra (C) and XRD pattern (D) of the GQDs.
The XPS (A), C1s (B), O1s (C), and N1s (D) spectra of the as-obtained GQDs.
(A) Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs. Inset: photographs of GQDs under daylight (1) and UV light (2). (B) Emission spectra of GQDs for different excitation wavelengths.

(A) The influence of pH values on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. (B) Photographs of the influence of pH values on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs under daylight (1) and UV light (2) (Number 1 shows blank and number 2–12 show pH value 2–12).

(A) Effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. (B) The relationship between F/F 0 and Fe3+ form 1 to 250μM. Inset: The linear range of the sensing assay. (C) Photographs of the effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs under daylight (1) and UV light (2) (Number 1 shows blank and number 2 to 11 show metal ions: Hg2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ag+).
Viability of the SW480 after 24h incubation with the GQDs (mean±SD, n=3).

Confocal fluorescence images of SW480 cells incubated for 4h in the absence and presence of GQDs. (A–C) shows confocal fluorescence images of SW480 cells in the absence of GQDs. (A) The bright field image of SW480 cells. (B) The fluorescence image SW480 cells with 405nm excitation. (C) Merged image of SW480 cells. (D–F) shows that confocal fluorescence images of SW480 cells in the presence of GQDs. (D) The bright field image of SW480 cells. (E) The fluorescence image SW480 cells labeled with GQDs with 405nm excitation. (F) Merged image of SW480 cells incubated with GQDs.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process for the GQDs.
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Microwave assisted one-pot synthesis of graphene quantum dots as highly sensitive fluorescent probes for detection of iron ions and pH value

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Opto-electronic Technology, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon nanomaterials have received considerable attention as fluorescent probes owing to their low toxicity, water solubility and stable photochemical properties. However, the development of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is still on its early stage. In this work, GQDs were successfully synthesized by one-step microwave assisted pyrolysis of aspartic acid (Asp) and NH4HCO3 mixture. The as-prepared GQDs exhibited strongly blue fluorescence with high quantum yield up to 14%. Strong fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on GQDs can be used for its high selectivity detection among of general metal ions. The probe exhibited a wide linear response concentration range (0–50μM) to Fe3+ and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.26μM. In addition, GQDs are also sensitive to the pH value in the range from 2 to 12 indicating a great potential as optical pH sensors. More importantly, the GQDs possess lower cellular toxicity and high photostability and can be directly used as fluorescent probes for cell imaging.
Aspartic acid-derived graphene quantum dots show great sensing property for Fe3+, pH value and bioimaging as fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron ions play a crucial role in the biological system and either deficiency or excess of iron will result in some diseases, such as anemia, heart failure, diabetes and cancer [1–3]. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ is important for the early diagnosis of these diseases. Up to now, a few methods for tracking Fe3+ have been reported such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [4], atomic absorption spectrometry [5], and spectrophotometry [6], etc. However, these instrumental techniques require sophisticated instrumentation and tedious sample preparation procedures, which limit the practical applications in routine Fe3+ detection. Graphitic carbon quantum dots [1], and fluorescent graphene oxide [7] as fluorescent Fe3+ sensors have been reported. Therefore, the development of a novel fluorescent probe with low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and high water solubility has become increasingly important and urgent.

In addition, the sensitive monitoring of pH is also very important. The pH need to be monitored continuously in biomedical, chemical and environmental analyses [8,9]. Monitoring the pH changes plays a critical role in many physiological, pathological processes and in chemical media. Several methods to measure pH have been reported, including organic dyes [10], semiconductor quantum dots [11], carbon dots [12] and graphene quantum dots [13]. Among these developed methods, fluorescence-based techniques display high rapidity, sensitivity and applicability. So they have drawn much attention because.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a newcomers of fluorescent nanomaterials in the carbon family, have generated much excitement because of their superiority in chemical inertness, low photobleaching, low toxicity, environmentally friendly, ease of functionalization and excellent biocompatibility [14–17]. Hence, GQDs have been regarded as promising candidates of semiconductor quantum dots in biological application and chemical analysis [15,18–20]. GQDs are 0 D graphene sheets with lateral sizes less than 100nm, displaying unique optical and electronic properties due to their quantum confinement and edge effects, which are similar to carbon quantum dots [14,15,21–23]. However, carbon quantum dots possess size effects, and the size of carbon quantum dots is less than 10nm. Therefore, GQDs and their chemical derivatives have received more attention [14,24]. GQDs, as a kind of C-dots, have been widely synthesized by hydrothermal [25,26], organic synthesis [27], microwave assisted [28], and so on. Compared to the mentioned synthetic methods, microwave assisted pyrolysis would significantly reduce the fabrication time and obviously improve product yields and purities [29]. The microwave assisted pyrolysis carbonization of different carbohydrates has been reported to fabricate GQDs [28]. GQDs prepared by most of these methods mentioned above yield λex-dependent emission. Multicolor bioimaging might be obtained by exciting at different wavelengths [29].

In this study, we present a rapid microwave-assisted one-step method for preparing water soluble GQDs ( Scheme 1). The as synthesized GQDs possess lower cellular toxicity and high photostability, and can be directly used as fluorescent probes for cell imaging. Furthermore, the GQDs solution emits strong blue fluorescence, which is quite sensitive not only to the pH value in the range from 2 to 12 but also to iron ions. This endows them with the great potential as optical pH sensors and iron ion colorimetric detector. Iron ions play a crucial role in the biological system and either deficiency or excess of iron will result in some diseases. The highly sensitive and feasible detection of iron ions is also an important issue.

Aspartic acid (Asp) was purchased from Japan. NH4HCO3 was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All glassware was washed with aqua regia (conc. HCl: conc. HNO3, volume ratio=3:1), and then rinsed with ultrapure water and ethanol. The water used throughout all the experiments was purified through a Millipore system. CCK-8 kit was purchased from BOSTER. ISCOVE's Modified Dulbecco's Medium (IMDM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Gibco. SW480 cells were purchased from Cell Resource Center, IBMS, CAMS/PUMS.

GQDs were synthesized by microwave assisted pyrolysis method using the following procedure. 1g Asp and 2g NH4HCO3 were added to 20mL of deionized water a beaker. The beaker was then heated using microwave irradiation for 10min in a microwave reactor (MCR-3, Yuhua, China) operating at a power of 560W. The obtained GQDs were further purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a membrane with dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off=500) for 7h to obtain the final GQDs.

The size and morphology of GQDs were measured by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on JEM-1200EX electron microscopy with an accelerating voltage of 100kV. For TEM characterization, one drop of GQDs suspension was deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of GQDs was obtained using Cu Kα radiation (k=1.54056A˚). Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on an IFS 66 V/S (Bruker) IR spectrometer in the range of 400–4000cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were collected by a Thermo escalab 250Xi. The photoluminescence spectra were recorded on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan). QY was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.05M sulfuric acid solution (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 310nm) as a standard for GQDs. The optical densities measured on UV–vis spectra were acquired on a UV-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) using a 1cm path length cuvette. Absolute values were calculated using the standard reference sample that has a fixed and known fluorescence QY value.
The QY of the as -synthesized GQDs was calculated by measuring the fluorescence intensity in aqueous dispersion based on the following equation,

where Q is the quantum yield, I is the measured intensity of luminescent spectra, A is the optical density at excitation wavelength. QY was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.05M sulfuric acid solution (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 310nm) as a standard for GQDs. The subscript “R” refers to standard with known quantum yield and “GQD” for the sample in this equation. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength (350nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of metal ions and pH on the PL of the GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To the best of our knowledge, there are usually co-existing common metal ions in urine and pharmaceutical preparations. To address the question whether the foreign coexisting substances have effect on the measurement of the GQDs or not, further interference experiments were performed in the presence of the GQDs together with the common metal ions. For the detection of metal ions, various metal ions aqueous solutions including Fe3+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ag+ were first prepared and used as different metal ion sources. The PL spectra of GQDs in the absence and presence of various metal ions were recorded upon a 350nm excitation.

The sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions was executed in aqueous solution at room temperature. A series of Fe3+ aqueous solutions with various concentrations was freshly prepared before use. To evaluate the sensitivity towards Fe3+, a series of concentrations of Fe3+ were added into the aqueous solution containing the same amount of GQDs. Then the quenching effects of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of the GQDs were examined. The PL spectra were recorded by operating the fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 350nm.

The cytotoxicity of GQDs towards SW480 cells was evaluated by the CCK-8 method. The SW480 cells were seeded into 96 wells culture plate and then incubated overnight in an incubator. After the culture medium was removed, GQDs with different concentrations were added into each well. Cells were then allowed to incubate for another 24h. Then, CCK-8 (10µl per well) solution was added in each well and incubated at 37°C for 1h. The absorbance was measured at 450nm by the Enzyme standard instrument.

SW480 cells were cultured in IMDM medium containing GQDs in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator at 37°C for 6h. Then the cells were washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS), mixed with 4% paraformaldehyde in PBS at 4°C for 30min to fix the state of cells, washed again with PBS twice, and observed with an NIKON TE2000 inverted fluorescence microscope system. Confocal luminescence imaging of SW480 cells was performed with a modified Olympus FV1000 laser-scanning microscope. A 60 × oil-immersion objective lens was used.

The morphology and structure of the as-prepared GQDs under the optimal microwave-assisted polyol synthesis conditions were investigated by HRTEM and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. 1A shows the TEM images of the GQDs dispersed in water. As displayed from the TEM image, the diameters of GQDs were mainly distributed in the narrow range of 1.8–2.4nm with an average size of 2.1nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, HRTEM images indicate the high crystallinity of the GQDs and the lattice parameters are 0.33nm and 0.25nm, respectively, taking into account that the basal plane distance of bulk graphite is 0.335nm [26,30,31]. These results indicated that the one-pot microwave assisted method was efficient for the fabrication of GQDs. The XRD patterns of the as-prepared GQDs are displayed in Fig. 1D. The diffraction peak is at the range of 21.30–24.25°,which corresponds to a graphitic structure [25,26,32]. The small size of GQDs lead to the broadening of the diffraction peak [26].

The surface functional groups present on the as-prepared GQDs were characterized by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1C). The absorption bands at 3000–3500cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of O⎔–H and N–H. The peak at 1400cm−1 was from the bending vibration of C–NH and indicated the successful doping of nitrogen atoms into the GQDs. The band at 1713cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of C=C, the peaks at 1600cm−1 related to the C=O stretching vibrations, and the peaks at 1213cm−1 related to the C–O–C stretching vibrations, implying the oxygen-rich property of the GQDs. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of the GQDs in an aqueous system, which accounted for the great potential for sensing in aqueous samples.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further confirm the functional groups on the surface of the resultant GQDs. The results clearly reveal three peaks at 284.6, 401.2, and 531.2eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. A high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s ( Fig. 2B) confirmed the presence of C=C (sp2, 284.6eV), C–N (286.0eV), C=O (288.2eV) bonds [33–35]. The results showed that the resultant GQDs were rich in hydrophilic groups on the surfaces, which was in good agreement with FTIR spectrum. As displayed in Fig. 2C, the peaks located at 401.2 and 399.8eV in the N1s spectrum indicate two forms of nitrogen in the form of N–H and C–N, respectively [34,36]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) showed the two fitted peaks at 531.2 and 532.3eV, which are assigned to C=O and C–OH/C–O–C groups, respectively [34]. The surface components of the GQDs confirmed by the XPS were consistent with the above corresponding FTIR results.

The remarkable optical properties of the as-obtained GQDs were investigated by the UV–vis absorption and the fluorescent spectra of the aqueous dispersion of the GQDs under different excitation wavelengths, as displayed in Fig. 3A. The as-obtained GQDs exhibited a broad UV–vis absorption, which was attributed to the n–π* transition of GQDs [37–39]. In addition, the solution of GQDs emits bright blue luminescence under the irradiation of UV-light (365nm) (Fig. 3A). As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence emission wavelength has a red-shift at various excitation wavelengths from 285 to 450nm and the maximum emission fluorescence located at 442nm is excited by a laser of 350nm. To date, the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior of GQDs remains debated and required further elucidation. However, it was generally agreed the different surface status of GQDs mainly affected the fluorescence properties [37,40]. In addition, nanoparticles with different sizes also contributed to the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior of GQDs [37]. The as-obtained GQDs exhibited narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectra profiles, possibly resulting from their narrower size distribution. The quantum yield (QY) of GQDs was measured to be 14%, which can be used as a highly sensitive chemosensor. The N-doping induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of the GQDs possibly resulted in the highly efficient fluorescence emission [37]. These results indicated that the microwave assisted method was a promising approach to fabricate GQDs with high performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of GQDs toward pH
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the influence of pH value on fluorescence intensity of GQDs, fluorescence intensity of the GQDs was measured in the pH range of 2–12. Fig. 4A demonstrated the fluorescence intensities of GQDs at different pH values. The fluorescence intensity obviously decayed from pH=2–12, but the peak position was nearly unchanged from pH=2–6, and the peak position from pH 7–12 red shift from 458 to 491nm under excitation at 350nm. The phenomenon might be caused the protonated and deprotonated of function group on the surface of GQDs [9]. The change of fluorescence intensity and fluorescence color with pH from 6 to 7, which may make GQDs be applied in the biological field. The photographs of GQDs in the pH range of 2–12 were shown in Fig. 4B (daylight (1) and UV light (2)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of GQDs on Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, the influence of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was also investigated by recording the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of representative metal ions, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ag+. As displayed in Fig. 5A, there appears an obvious quenching of fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe3+. Previous reports had already revealed that hydroxyl displayed good binding affinity for Fe3+ [41,42]. Thus, the reason leading to fluorescence quenching might be explained as follows. The complexation between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl of GQDs occurred, which resulted in electron transferred to the d orbital of Fe3+ [40,41,43]. However, no obvious change in fluorescence intensity was observed in the presence of other metal ions, indicating that GQDs could be developed as a selective sensor for the detection of Fe3+. The photographs of GQDs in the presence and absence of representative metal ions were shown in Fig. 5C (daylight (1) and UV light (2)).

Next, we measured the detection limit of Fe3+ and the experiments were carried out by adding various concentrations of Fe3+ at 0–250μM. Upon the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs gradually decays at 442nm. The quenching efficiency exhibited a good linear relationship ay the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–50μM (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.26μM. The obtained limit of detection of the GQDs sensor for Fe3+ detection is much lower than those previously reported with other different fluorescent probes, as shown in Table 1 [44–47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of GQDs and the application in cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: For future biological applications, the potential cytotoxicity of the GQDs was evaluated using SW480 cells through the CCK-8 method. The cell viabilities were analyzed upon exposure to 0–1000μg/ml concentrations of the GQDs. The result indicates that these GQDs have very low cytotoxicity as shown in Fig. 6. Thus, these GQDs had great potential to replace the conventional semiconductor QDs to be applied in the biological bioimaging field or bioanalysis field.

We further investigated the bioimaging performance of GQDs as fluorescent probes in SW480 cells. SW480 cells were treated with GQDs for 4h to demonstrate their possibility as bioimaging agents. Cellular uptake of these GQDs was examined by confocal microscopy. As shown in the bright field image of SW480 cells treated with GQDs ( Fig. 7D), the normal morphology of SW480 cells further verify that these GQDs were very low toxicity. As expected, a remarkable intra-cellular blue fluorescence is observed by excitation at the wavelength of 405nm after treatment with the GQDs for 4h (Fig. 7E). In contrast, no visible fluorescence was detected in the control experiments without the GQDs involved under the same conditions (Fig. 7B). The merged image (Fig. 7F) further shows the ability of GQDs to penetrate into the cell membrane without any further surface passivation. All these results confirmed that the as synthesized GQDs from Asp can serve as a promising candidate for bioimaging agents for living cells.

Water soluble GQDs have been successfully synthesized through a microwave-assisted one-step method using Asp and NH4HCO3 as the reactants. The as synthesized GQDs possessed lower cellular toxicity and high photostability, and can be directly used as fluorescent probes for cell imaging. Furthermore, the GQDs solution showed strong blue fluorescence which was quite sensitive not only to the pH value but also to iron ions, endowing them with the great potential as optical pH sensors and iron ion colorimetric detector.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the State Key Project of Fundamental Research (Grants 2014CB931900 and 2012CB932504), the Chinese Academy of Sciences (“Hundred Talents Project” and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (119103S240)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.12.015.
